coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of GO/CDots/BiOI nanocomposites with enhanced photocatalytic 4-chlorophenol degradation and mechanism insight
coredata.dc:creator: Qu, Songying
Xiong, Yuhan
Zhang, Jun
coredata.dc:description: Non-metallic graphene oxide (GO) and carbon nanodots (CDots) co-doped BiOI ternary system (GO/CDots/BiOI) was successfully synthesized via a simple one-step solvothermal process. The compositional characterization, optical and electrical properties of photocatalysts were investigated in detail. The prepared ternary photocatalysts possessed the excellent visible-light driven photocatalytic 4-chlorophenol (4-CP) degradation. Additionally, the 4-CP removal efficiencies decreased in the order of GO/CDots/BiOI (98.2%) > CDots/BiOI (89.9%) > GO/BiOI (78.5%) > pristine BiOI (41.7%) in 3 h under visible light irradiation. The dissolved organic carbon (DOC) removal and the dechlorination efficiency by the GO/CDots/BiOI were 66.3% and 85.2%, respectively, much higher than pristine BiOI. The co-existence of GO and CDots on the BiOI greatly promoted visible light harvesting and utilizing ability and inhibited the recombination of photogenerated electron/hole pairs. The synergistic effect between GO, CDots and BiOI was expounded, and the photocatalytic reaction mechanism was proposed in detail via the band structure analysis and free radical trapping experiments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: BiOI
Non-metallic co-catalyst
Synergistic effect
4-Chlorophenol
Photocatalysis

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Laboratory of Groundwater Resources & Environment, Ministry of Education, Jilin University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by Key Laboratory of Groundwater Resources & Environment, Ministry of Education, Jilin University, Changchun 130021, Jilin, China .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM (a and b), TEM (c) and HRTEM (d) images and EDX mapping (e–i) of the as-prepared GO/CDots/BiOI photocatalysts.

XRD patterns (a) of as-prepared BiOI, GO/BiOI, CDots/BiOI and GO/CDots/BiOI samples; FT-IR spectra (b) of BiOI and GO/CDots/BiOI; High-resolution XPS spectra of the GO/CDots/BiOI nanocomposites: I 3d (c), Bi 4f (d), O 1s (e) and C 1s (f).

UV–Vis DRS (a) and calculated the band gaps (b) of as-prepared the BiOI, GO/BiOI, CDots/BiOI GO/CDots/BiOI; Valence band XPS spectra (c) and band structure diagram (d) of the BiOI and GO/CDots/BiOI photocatalysts.
UV–Vis–NIR absorption spectrum of CDots. Inset is the up-converted PL spectra of CDots with different excitation wavelengths from 700 to 1000 nm with the interval of 50 nm.
Transient photocurrent curves (a), EIS (b), PL spectra (c) of BiOI, CDots/BiOI, GO/BiOI, GO/CDots/BiOI photocatalysts.

Photocatalytic degradation of 4-CP (a), the pseudo-first-order reaction kinetic model (b) of the as-prepared photocatalysts. The released Cl- and DOC removal efficiency for 4-CP of the GO/CDots/BiOI (c), cycling test of GO/CDots/BiOI (d).

Free radical trapping experiments of active-species during the photocatalytic degradation of 4-CP (a) Schematic photocatalytic mechanism of the separation and transfer of photogenerated charges (b) in the GO/CDots/BiOI system under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of GO/CDots/BiOI nanocomposites with enhanced photocatalytic 4-chlorophenol degradation and mechanism insight

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Environmental Science & Engineering Research Center, Shenzhen Graduate School, Harbin Institute of Technology, Shenzhen 518055, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Groundwater Resources & Environment, Ministry of Education, Jilin University, Changchun 130021, Jilin, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Groundwater Resources & Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Groundwater Resources & Environment, Ministry of Education, Jilin University, Changchun 130021, Jilin, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Groundwater Resources & Environment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Non-metallic graphene oxide (GO) and carbon nanodots (CDots) co-doped BiOI ternary system (GO/CDots/BiOI) was successfully synthesized via a simple one-step solvothermal process. The compositional characterization, optical and electrical properties of photocatalysts were investigated in detail. The prepared ternary photocatalysts possessed the excellent visible-light driven photocatalytic 4-chlorophenol (4-CP) degradation. Additionally, the 4-CP removal efficiencies decreased in the order of GO/CDots/BiOI (98.2%) > CDots/BiOI (89.9%) > GO/BiOI (78.5%) > pristine BiOI (41.7%) in 3 h under visible light irradiation. The dissolved organic carbon (DOC) removal and the dechlorination efficiency by the GO/CDots/BiOI were 66.3% and 85.2%, respectively, much higher than pristine BiOI. The co-existence of GO and CDots on the BiOI greatly promoted visible light harvesting and utilizing ability and inhibited the recombination of photogenerated electron/hole pairs. The synergistic effect between GO, CDots and BiOI was expounded, and the photocatalytic reaction mechanism was proposed in detail via the band structure analysis and free radical trapping experiments.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic techniques had attracted explosive attention since it could utilize the solar energy to degrade the contaminants efficiently, as well as its low cost, innocuous, environment-friendly and easily available nature [1]. The diverse photocatalysts were studied for the degradation of organic pollutants under Ultra Violet (UV) or visible light irradiation such as TiO2, ZnO, WO3, g-C3N4 ZnFe2O4 and Bi2WO6 [1–5]. Iodine bismuth oxide (BiOI) had unique layered structure built by interlacing [Bi2O2]2+ slabs with double I atoms, which was benefit to form internal static electric fields between I positive and I negative layer and then enhanced separation of the photo-generated electron/hole pairs [6–8]. Besides, the photo-excited electrons should travel a certain k-space distance to the valence band due to the indirect-transition band-gap of BiOI, leading to the low recombination probability of the electron/hole pairs [9–11]. Nevertheless, the photocatalytic performance of unitary BiOI was still limited owing to the low solar energy utilization efficiency and rapid electron/hole pairs recombination [12–14].

In recent years, carbon nanodots (CDots, a carbon nanomaterial less than 10 nm in diameter) attracted the interests of researchers due to the unique characteristics over conventional semiconductor-based dots including water-soluble, intrinsic low toxicity, good biocompatibility, photostable and nanosized light-harvester [15–17]. The conjugated π structure of the CDots made them excellent electron reservoir and transporter [18]. Simultaneously, the up-conversion properties of CDots enabled the light from the longer wavelength to shorter wavelength, improving the utilization of solar light [16]. These properties endowed CDots as a co-catalyst to decorate many semiconductors to accelerate the separation-transportation process of photo-induced carriers within the semiconductors and improve their interfacial reduction/oxidation reactions [4,16,17]. Besides, some researchers have also tried to couple semiconductors with graphene oxide (GO), a promising carbon material, which possesses high surface area, excellent electrical conductivity, rapid heterogeneous electron transfer and outstanding mechanical properties to enhance its electron conduction and transfer behavior to restrain recombination of photogenerated electron/hole pairs [19–22].

Recently, the ternary photocatalysts with GO and CDots co-dopants have received much attention owing to much higher photocatalytic performance compared to the binary photocatalysts [23]. However, limited work has been done on the mechanism insight to the combined effect of GO and CDots on the surface of semiconductors. In this work, we fabricated the GO/CDots/BiOI ternary photocatalyst to dramatically improve the photocatalytic activity compared to the pristine BiOI and the binary system. The crystal structures, morphologies and optical and electrochemical properties were investigated in detail. The photocatalytic activities of the GO/CDots/BiOI microspheres were tested by the degradation of 4-CP. The effect of GO and CDots on the band structure of BiOI microspheres was intensively studied. The synergistic effect between GO, CDots and BiOI was investigated. Accordingly, the photocatalytic mechanism of the separation and transfer of photo-generated charges on the GO/CDots/BiOI ternary system was proposed and elucidated under visible light illumination.

All reagents were used directly without further treatment. The details of the chemicals were listed in Table S1. The CDots was prepared via a typical electrochemical method [17]. The GO/CDots/BiOI were synthesized by a mixed precipitation method according to previous reports [12,24]. In a typical procedure, 4 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved into a solution contained 36 mL deionized water and 4 mL acetic acid to obtain solution A. 4 mmol IL [C16mim]I, 2 mL CDots (4 mg/L) and 4 mL GO (5 mg/L) aqueous were all dispersed into 40 mL alcohol to obtain solution B. The solution B was added into solution A dropwise under stirring condition and then the mixing solution was stirred for another 1 h. Subsequently, the resultant product was centrifuged and washed adequately with alcohol and deionized water three times. Finally, the resulting precipitates were dried in a vacuum at 60 °C overnight and ground into fine powder before further characterization and using in photocatalytic reactions. For comparison, the pure BiOI, GO/BiOI and CDots/BiOI were also prepared via the same approach by only adding nothing, GO and CDots, respectively.
Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2018.08.027.

All reagents were used directly without further treatment. The details of the chemicals were listed in Table S1 . The CDots was prepared via a typical electrochemical method [17]. The GO/CDots/BiOI were synthesized by a mixed precipitation method according to previous reports [12,24]. In a typical procedure, 4 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved into a solution contained 36 mL deionized water and 4 mL acetic acid to obtain solution A. 4 mmol IL [C16mim]I, 2 mL CDots (4 mg/L) and 4 mL GO (5 mg/L) aqueous were all dispersed into 40 mL alcohol to obtain solution B. The solution B was added into solution A dropwise under stirring condition and then the mixing solution was stirred for another 1 h. Subsequently, the resultant product was centrifuged and washed adequately with alcohol and deionized water three times. Finally, the resulting precipitates were dried in a vacuum at 60 °C overnight and ground into fine powder before further characterization and using in photocatalytic reactions. For comparison, the pure BiOI, GO/BiOI and CDots/BiOI were also prepared via the same approach by only adding nothing, GO and CDots, respectively.

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM-7001F), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, 200 kV FEI-Tecnai F20) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) were used to investigate the morphology and structure of as-prepared catalysts. The chemical composition was investigated via energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDX). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were conducted on a Bruker D8 powder X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα (λ = 0.15406 nm) radiation. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained via a VERTEX70 spectrophotometer (Bruker, Germany) using pressed KBr discs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was detected via a VG ESCALAB210 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) XPS system using an Al Kα (h = 1486.6 eV) radiation excitation source. All obtained spectra were calibrated against the C 1s signal at 284.6 eV. UV–Vis diffuse reflection spectra (DRS) were taken on a Lambda 750 spectrophotometer (Perking Elmer). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on a luminescence spectrometer (Cary Eclipse, Varian Medical Systems, USA).

A 150 W Xenon lamp (CEL-HXF 300, Beijing Education Au-light Co., Ltd.) equipped with a UV cutoff filter (λ > 420 nm) was chosen as the light source, and the spectrum was shown in Fig. S1. In a typical experiment, 0.1 g photocatalysts were fully dissolved in 100 mL 4-CP (20 mg L−1) aqueous solution. Prior to irradiation, the suspension was adequately stirring for 1 h in dark to reach adsorption/desorption equilibrium. In the process of irradiation, 0.5 mL suspension was sampled regularly and filtered by a 0.45 µm membrane before detection. The stability and repeatability of photocatalysts were measured by repeating the identical experiment for 5 times.

A 150 W Xenon lamp (CEL-HXF 300, Beijing Education Au-light Co., Ltd.) equipped with a UV cutoff filter (λ >420 nm) was chosen as the light source, and the spectrum was shown in Fig. S1. In a typical experiment, 0.1 g photocatalysts were fully dissolved in 100 mL 4-CP (20 mg L−1) aqueous solution. Prior to irradiation, the suspension was adequately stirring for 1 h in dark to reach adsorption/desorption equilibrium. In the process of irradiation, 0.5 mL suspension was sampled regularly and filtered by a 0.45 µm membrane before detection. The stability and repeatability of photocatalysts were measured by repeating the identical experiment for 5 times.

The 4-CP concentration was determined by an Agilent 1100 HPLC equipped with a 4.6 × 250 mm, 5 μm Venusil HILIC column, and the wavelength of the UV detector was set at 280 nm. The mobile phase (v/v) consisted of ultrapure water (30%) and acetonitrile (70%) at a flow rate of 1.0 mL min−1. The analyses of Cl− was conducted on an ion-exchange chromatography (ICS-2100, Dionex Inc., USA). The change of dissolved organic carbon (DOC) was carried out via a total organic carbon analyzer (SSM-5000A, Shimadzu Company).

The scavenger quenching experiments were carried out in the same condition except the addtion of the scavengers. The details were sodium chromate (VI) (0.05 mM, Sigma, 99.5%) for electron, sodium oxalate (0.5 mM, Sigma, 99.5%) for hole, isopropanol (0.5 mM, Sigma, 99.5%) for hydroxyl radical (
OH) and 2,2,6,6-Tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy (TEMPO) for superoxide radical (
O2 −) (1 mM, Sigma, 99%). The volume ratio of scavenger and 4-CP was 1:100.

The transient photo-current and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were measured via a CHI-660e electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua, China). A Pt plate and an Ag/AgCl electrode were used as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The as-prepared photocatalysts were spin-coated (2800 rpm) onto an FTO glass to act as the working electrode. 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution was used as the electrolyte.

The surface morphology of the as-synthesized GO/CDots/BiOI materials was detected by the field emission SEM, TEM and HRTEM. In Fig. 1 a, it can be seen that the as-prepared composites presented the sphere-like structure with an average diameter of about 1–2 μm with the GO evenly added. The high-magnification SEM image illustrated that the edge of sphere-like structure was constructed of plentiful ultrathin nanosheets (Fig. 1b), and the part of surface was covered by GO. The unique porous structure with the accumulation of nanosheets favored the faster interfacial charge transfer, which was benefit for the photodegradation [25,26]. The formation of GO/CDots/BiOI nanocomposite was further proved by TEM and HRTEM (Fig. 1c and d). The observed lattice fringe spacing of 0.282 nm matched the (1 1 0) crystallographic plane of BiOI crystal [27]. The lattice fringe spacing with d = 0.212 nm was in accordance with the crystal plane of CDots (1 0 1) facet, demonstrating the successful coupling of the CDots with BiOI [17,18]. Additionally, the lamellar GO could be observed clearly. These results intimated that GO and CDots were uniformly dispersed on the surface of BiOI. As shown in Fig. 1e–i, the EDX mapping clearly presented the elements of Bi, O, I and C were well distributed on the as-synthesized GO/CDots/BiOI materials. Therefore, the above results together demonstrated that the GO and CDots were successfully introduced on the surface of BiOI materials [13,28,29].

The crystal structure and phase composition of the as-prepared BiOI, GO/BiOI, CDots/BiOI and GO/CDots/BiOI materials were characterized by XRD (Fig. 2 a). The positions of peaks for all the samples were readily indexed to the structure of the tetragonal phase of BiOI (JCPDS card No. 10-0445) [24,30]. No changes appeared in the XRD diffraction peaks of BiOI after the addition of GO or/and CDots. This meant that the GO and CDots were only deposited on the surfaces of BiOI, rather than entering into the lattice internal and influencing the growth of BiOI [15,19]. No obvious characteristic diffraction peaks of CDots and GO could be found in the GO/BiOI, CDots/BiOI and GO/CDots/BiOI, which might be ascribed to its low loading amount and uniform dispersion in the samples that may be beyond the XRD detection limit [4,20]. No extra characteristic peaks from possible impurities could be observed, indicating the high purity and single phase of the as prepared composites [15].

Fig. 2b displayed the FT-IR spectra of the as-prepared BiOI and GO/CDots/BiOI. The absorption bands at around 518 cm−1 was ascribed to the BiO stretching mode [28,31]. The bands peak at about 1416 cm−1 might have been caused by the existence of OH, COH and COO [15]. The absorption peak located at 1654 cm−1 might be due to the CO bending vibrations [23]. These results indicated that the existence of COOH and OH groups on the surface of GO and CDots [20]. The absorption peak at 3240 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of OH of adsorbed water molecules [26].

To illustrate the surface elemental compositions and their valence states, the XPS analysis of the GO/CDots/BiOI composite was carried out. The detailed fitting parameters of XPS spectra were summarized in Table S2. From I 3d spectra (Fig. 2c), it can be found that two strong peaks of the spin–orbit splitting energy at 618.1 eV and 630.9 eV, which were associated to I 3d5/2 and I 3d3/2, respectively, which verified that the bromine species were I− anions [7]. Fig. 2d shows the high-resolution XPS spectrum of the Bi 4f spectra, where two peaks at 158.2 eV and 164.3 eV could be indexed with Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, respectively [13,32,33]. Meanwhile the binding energy between these two peaks was 6.1 eV, indicating the presence of the Bi3+ in the composite, which agreed well with previous reports [12,33]. For the O 1s (Fig. 2e), two peaks at about 530.0 eV and 530.9 eV could be ascribed to the oxygen in Bi2O2 2+ and BiO chemical bonding in the BiOI, respectively [10]. The other peaks centered at 531.8 eV, 532.5 eV and 533.7 eV binding energies, corresponded to different types of oxygen molecule such as hydroxyl groups, crystal water, carboxyl groups [24]. As shown in Fig. 2f, the peak of C 1s binding energies at 284.6, 285.0 and 285.5 eV can be ascribed to sp2 CC/CC bonds, CO/CO and COC bond, indicating the formation of GO and CDots [17,19,20].

To illustrate the surface elemental compositions and their valence states, the XPS analysis of the GO/CDots/BiOI composite was carried out. The detailed fitting parameters of XPS spectra were summarized in Table S2. From I 3d spectra (Fig. 2c), it can be found that two strong peaks of the spin–orbit splitting energy at 618.1 eV and 630.9 eV, which were associated to I 3d5/2 and I 3d3/2, respectively, which verified that the bromine species were I− anions [7]. Fig. 2d shows the high-resolution XPS spectrum of the Bi 4f spectra, where two peaks at 158.2 eV and 164.3 eV could be indexed with Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, respectively [13,32,33]. Meanwhile the binding energy between these two peaks was 6.1 eV, indicating the presence of the Bi3+ in the composite, which agreed well with previous reports [12,33]. For the O 1s (Fig. 2e), two peaks at about 530.0 eV and 530.9 eV could be ascribed to the oxygen in Bi2O2 2+ and BiO chemical bonding in the BiOI, respectively [10]. The other peaks centered at 531.8 eV, 532.5 eV and 533.7 eV binding energies, corresponded to different types of oxygen molecule such as hydroxyl groups, crystal water, carboxyl groups [24]. As shown in Fig. 2f, the peak of C 1s binding energies at 284.6, 285.0 and 285.5 eV can be ascribed to sp2 CC/CC bonds, CO/CO and COC bond, indicating the formation of GO and CDots [17,19,20].

The Raman spectra of the GO/CDots/BiOI nanocomposites (Fig. S2) contained two characteristic peaks that were not present in the spectra of the BiOI, located at about 1332.2 and 1606.3 cm−1, corresponding to the D-band and the G-band, respectively [21,34]. The D-band arose from the vibrations of carbon atoms in disordered graphite, while the G-band was attributed to an ordered graphite structure [29,34]. This indicated that the BiOI crystal structure of the GO/CDots/BiOI nanocomposites contained considerable amounts of defects and lattice disorders corresponding to GO and CDots, demonstrating the successful chemical combination of these three species [34].

The Raman spectra of the GO/CDots/BiOI nanocomposites (Fig. S2) contained two characteristic peaks that were not present in the spectra of the BiOI, located at about 1332.2 and 1606.3 cm−1, corresponding to the D-band and the G-band, respectively [21,34]. The D-band arose from the vibrations of carbon atoms in disordered graphite, while the G-band was attributed to an ordered graphite structure [29,34]. This indicated that the BiOI crystal structure of the GO/CDots/BiOI nanocomposites contained considerable amounts of defects and lattice disorders corresponding to GO and CDots, demonstrating the successful chemical combination of these three species [34].

The surface area and pore structure of GO/CDots/BiOI were investigated by the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm (Fig. S3). Additionally, the pore size distribution of the GO/CDots/BiOI was determined using the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method (Fig. S3) [35]. According to IUPAC classification of mesoporous materials, the as-prepared samples with the hysteresis loop in the P/P0 range of 0.00–1.00 exhibited typical type IV isotherm [36]. As shown in Fig. S3, the mesopores in the size range of 2–50 nm were observed, which might be ascribed to ultrathin nanosheets of the sphere-like structure [37].

The surface area and pore structure of GO/CDots/BiOI were investigated by the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm (Fig. S3). Additionally, the pore size distribution of the GO/CDots/BiOI was determined using the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method (Fig. S3) [35]. According to IUPAC classification of mesoporous materials, the as-prepared samples with the hysteresis loop in the P/P0 range of 0.00–1.00 exhibited typical type IV isotherm [36]. As shown in Fig. S3, the mesopores in the size range of 2–50 nm were observed, which might be ascribed to ultrathin nanosheets of the sphere-like structure [37].

Fig. 3 a illustrated the UV–Vis DRS of as-prepared BiOI and GO/CDots/BiOI. The absorption band with the onset absorption band edge of pristine BiOI was approximately located at 635 nm due to the intrinsic band gap transition rather than the impurity level transition [27]. In contrast, the absorption of visible-light (635 to 800 nm) significantly increased for the ternary GO/CDots/BiOI. The absorption edges basically stay the same after the addition of GO and CDots. The results suggested that the introduction of GO and CDots enable the composites to absorb more visible light and to endow the formation of more photo-excited electron/hole pairs with improved the photocatalytic activity [26]. Fig. 3b showed the energy band gaps of the samples, which were calculated by the classical Tauc’s approach [24]. The band gap (Eg) of GO/CDots/BiOI (1.94 eV) was nearly same as BiOI (1.95 eV). The VB of BiOI and GO/CDots/BiOI were measured using XPS valence spectra (Fig. 3c) with the empirical equation [13,31]. The VB maximum positions (EVB) of pristine BiOI and GO/CDots/BiOI were determined to be both 2.25 eV, indicating that the modification of GO and CDots would not affect the electronic structure of BiOI. The existing form of GO and CDots were on the surface for charge transfer instead of inserting into the crystal lattice of BiOI [25]. According to the formula ECB = EVB − Eg and combined with the band gap [9], the CB of GO/CDots/BiOI and BiOI were 0.31 eV and 0.3 eV, respectively, as showed in Fig. 3d. Thus, the modification of GO and CDots effectively improved visible light harvesting and utilizing capability but did not change the band-gap structure of BiOI.

As shown in Fig. 4 , the as-prepared CDots possessed a strong, broad and continuous absorption in the UV, visible and even NIR regions. The up-converted PL spectra (Fig. 4 inset) indicated that the CDots could be excited by long-wavelength light from 700 to 1000 nm, with the emission wavelengths in the range from 350 to 580 nm. This feature of CDots was probably attributable to the multiphoton active process, which would effectively promote the charge transfer [38]. Thus, the nanocomposites containing CDots will definitely enhance the utilization of solar energy to generate electron/hole pairs [16].

Electrochemistry analysis and PL spectra were evaluated to explore the separation, migration, and trapping of photo-generated charge carriers. The photocurrent response curves of as-prepared samples were steady and reproducible during several on-off intermittent irradiation cycles (Fig. 5 a). The photocurrent density of GO/CDots/BiOI possessed the highest among all the samples, which indicated that the GO/CDots/BiOI composite present the excellent ability of photo-generated charge carrier separation and superior electronic conductivity. This might be attributed to the fact that the GO and CDots, as the photo-generated electrons acceptors and separation centers, enhanced the separation efficiency of the photo-excited electron-hole pairs under the light illumination [16,19,38]. Thus, the co-modification by CDots and GO could effectively improve separation of photo-generated charge carriers and boost the photocatalytic performance.

Further insight into the transfer properties of the photo-generated charge carriers can be proved by EIS (Fig. 5b). The semicircle radius for the prepared materials increased in the order GO/CDots/BiOI < GO/BiOI < CDots/BiOI < BiOI, suggesting that fastest separation efficiency of photo-induced electrons and holes could be achieved over the GO/CDots/BiOI electrode [6].

The steady-state PL spectra in Fig. 5c presented that composites displayed three obvious emission peaks centered at around 485, 530 and 570 nm under the excitation wavelength at 320 nm [7,13]. Apparently, the PL intensity of the GO/CDots/BiOI composite degreased significantly compared to the CDots/BiOI, GO/BiOI and pristine BiOI, revealing that CDots and GO could effectively prevent the recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes in GO/CDots/BiOI system [13,29]. The result of PL analysis was in accordance with the photocurrent and EIS analyses.

Fig. 6 a showed the comparison of photocatalytic 4-CP degradation efficiency of as-prepared catalysts. No appreciable change of 4-CP concentration in the negative control (no catalyst) demonstrated the photo-chemically stable of 4-CP [39]. As observed, the 4-CP removal efficiency in 3 h decreased in the order GO/CDots/BiOI (98.2%) > CDots/BiOI (89.9%) > GO/BiOI (78.5%) > pristine BiOI (41.7%). The degradation kinetics were conducted to quantitatively understand the degradation of 4-CP and further compare the photocatalytic activities of as-prepared catalysts [39]. As shown in Fig. 6b, the GO/CDots/BiOI possessed the highest apparent reaction rate constant (k value) over the samples, indicating its highest photocatalytic activity under the same conditions. As above, the calculated k values showed similar trends relative to 4-CP removal efficiency. Fig. 6c presented the dechlorination DOC removal efficiency in the entire photocatalytic 4-CP degradation process. As observed, the GO/CDots/BiOI exhibited a high released ratio of Cl− of ∼ 85.2% in 3 h. This suggested that the photocatalytic products were almost fully dechlorinated, which was conductive to further degradation [40]. Simultaneously, the mineralization efficiency of 4-CP was as high as ∼ 66.3%. Obviously, all the above experimental results further confirmed that the introduction of GO and CDots into GO/CDots/BiOI effectively promoted the separation efficiency of electron/hole pairs and boosted the photocatalytic performances.

In addition to the photocatalytic activity, the reusability and stability are a general requirement for the practical applications [41]. Following a simple washing and drying, the recycling reactions were examined by repeated photocatalytic experiments for the GO/CDots/BiOI composite. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency of 4-CP decreased slightly after five cycles (∼4.7%) (Fig. 6d), revealing that the composite possessed an excellent photostability.

The identification of active species in the GO/CDots/BiOI system during the 4-CP degradation were performed by free radical trapping experiments, as showed in Fig. 7 a. The obvious effect of the quenchers on removal efficiency of 4-CP decreased in the order of sodium oxalate (for h+) > TEMPOL (for
O2 −) > Cr (VI) (for e−) > isopropanol (for
OH). The result indicated that the active species of h+,
O2 −, e− and
OH played the critical role in the photo-degradation of 4-CP.

According to the DRS analysis (Fig. 3), the VB and CB position of the GO/CDots/BiOI composite were determined to be 2.25 eV and 0.31 eV, respectively. The standard redox potentials of
OH/OH− was 2.38 eV (vs. NHE) [41]. Thus, the photogenerated holes could not react with OH− to give rise to
OH radical (h+ + OH− =
OH) [25]. Theoretically, the CB of the prepared GO/CDots/BiOI nanocomposite was less positive than the standard reduction potential of O2/
O2 − (−0.046 eV vs. NHE) and
O2 − radical could also not be produced [15]. So, the
O2 − existed in system most likely been produced on the surface of the CDots and GO. The energetic electrons accumulated at the CDots and GO could be captured by O2 to generate
O2 − radical (O2 + e− =
O2 −) [41]. The generation
O2 − radical could be further converted to generate
OH radical (H2O +
O2 − →
OOH + OH−; H2O +
OOH + 2e− →
OH + 2OH−) [24]. The generation of the active species could effectively degrade the organic pollutants by the oxidation and reduction, which was consistent with the results of the free radical trapping experiments (Fig. 7a). As a result, the introduction of GO and CDots for BiOI made a great contribution to the enhancement in the photocatalytic activity.

In the basis of the above-mentioned results, the photocatalysis mechanisms of GO/CDots/BiOI ternary catalysts are proposed in Fig. 7b. Firstly, the CDots could also efficiently absorb longer-wavelength visible light, which was then transformed by up-conversion into shorter-wavelength light, which in turn was absorbed by the BiOI to produce additional photogenerated e−/h+ pairs. Hence, the entire spectrum of visible light energy could be utilized efficiently. The e− can be excited from the VB to the CB of BiOI with the visible light irradiation, leaving the h+ in the VB. At this moment, the electronic storage feature of GO would play a vital role. The e− in the CB of BiOI would be quickly transferred to GO through the contact interface due to their excellent electronic conductivity. The recombination of e−/h+ pairs could be effectively inhibited, extending the lifetime of the charge carriers. In this way, the e−/h+ pairs achieved spatial separation, and the oxidation and reduction processes separately occurred in different regions. This synergistic effect between GO, CDots and BiOI ensured the efficient photocatalytic activity of GO/CDots/BiOI nanocomposites. The 4-CP degradation occurs by a series of oxidation reactions. The first two products (4-chlorocatechol and 5-chloro-1,2,4-benzenetriol) are formed by hydroxylations of 4-CP. Oxidative cleavage of the aromatic ring produces the first diacid, and another oxidative cleavage releases CO2 and the second diacid. The two diacids would be further oxidized to CO2 and H2O.

A novel GO/CDots/BiOI ternary photocatalyst has been successfully fabricated via IL [C16mim]I assisted the solvothermal-reduction method. The GO/CDots/BiOI nanocomposites exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity (98.2%) with 66.3% in mineralization and 85.2% in dechlorination capacity for 4-CP decomposition under visible light irradiation. The ternary photocatalyst had the reusability and stability for practical applications. The GO as electron reservoir and transporter, prevent the recombination of photo-generated electron/hole pairs. In addition, the up-conversion properties of CDots improved the utilization of visible light. The GO and CDots co-doping on the BiOI did not change the band gap structure of BiOI in the ternary system. The mechanism for the GO/CDots/BiOI ternary photocatalyst with excellent photocatalytic performance was proposed and elucidated. These results suggest that GO/CDots/BiOI ternary system is a promising photocatalyst for removing various contaminants from water.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Key Laboratory of Groundwater Resources & Environment, Ministry of Education, Jilin University, Changchun 130021, Jilin, China.
